key_insight: A fine-tuned MACE potential can rapidly screen multicomponent transition-metal carbides for both synthesizability and hardness, and it uncovers previously unknown stacking-ordered phases in mixed group 4/5–6 compositions.
engineering_relevance: This enables high-throughput discovery of carbide coatings and extreme-environment materials with better chances of being both experimentally accessible and mechanically robust.
method: Fine-tuned a MACE machine-learned interatomic potential on ~28,000 DFT calculations across the group 4–6 metal–carbon composition space.
Screened 1500+ equiatomic compositions across rocksalt, hexagonal, and hcp prototypes using free-energy and elasticity-based hardness surrogates.
Validated stability trends with DFT and compared synthesizeability predictions at 1500°C against experimental reports.
evidence_signal: The model reaches about 10 meV/atom formation-energy error for thermodynamically relevant structures using only 20% of the training data, and its 1500°C synthesis predictions align well with experiments.
watch_item: The screening assumes a mostly disordered solid-solution picture; although short-range-order effects were small here, more complex local ordering or kinetic effects could still alter experimental outcomes.
paper_title: Synthesizability, hardness, and stacking order in multicomponent transition metal carbides from machine-learned potentials
